subject: [PATCH v1 08/17] fuse: add passthrough ops gating

reviews.author: Amir Goldstein
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Amir Goldstein noted that his library (libfuse_passthrough) uses kernel passthrough only if both read and write have been requested, suggesting that the current implementation may be too coarse-grained and proposing to extend the FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH API to allow for finer granularity.

Reviewer noted that FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH + 0 could be used to request passthrough to an existing backing inode, which might not align with the intended behavior of requesting passthrough for the lifetime of a file.

Reviewer noted that mmap operations cannot be sent to the server without using the page cache, which contradicts the original intention of preserving backwards compatibility.

Reviewer noted that the current implementation does not work for mmap operations because reads and writes are treated as separate logic paths, whereas user I/O is always a combination of read and write to the same memory space.

Reviewer noted that the passthrough ops check should follow the same per-file opt-out pattern as FOPEN_DIRECT_IO, and suggested making the implementation trivial by following its lead.
sentiment_signals: splitting reads from write logic does not work for mmap at all
reviews.raw_body:
I think it makes sense.
My internal customers have this use case (passthrough only read).

I have a library (libfuse_passthough) that implements fuse passthrough
template fs in userspace and this library currently uses kernel passthrough
only if both passthrough_read and passthrough_write have been requested:

https://github.com/amir73il/libfuse/blob/fuse_passthrough/passthrough/fuse_passthrough.cpp#L997

Note that the library provides this granularity at open time, not lookup
but we can also think about extending the FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH
api later to allow this.

---

I actually did not mean that, but now that I think about it,
FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH + backing_id could mean as today
"request passthrough for lifetime of file"
FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH + 0 could mean
"request passthrough to existing backing inode"

IIRC, the value of 0 as backing_id was intentionally reserved
for this purpose.

---

mmap cannot go to the server without page cache
and this is what we were trying to avoid.

---

Unfortunately not.
Splitting reads from write logic does not work for mmap at all.
User always read/write to the same memory, either mapped
to backing inode or to fuse page cache.
The logic of iomode.c is that if a backing inode exists mmap
always maps the backing file.

Server could request to get normal READ or normal WRITE or
both by not setting FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_OP_READ/WRITE in
ops mask, but it cannot opt-out of passthrough mmap.

---

I think it is a useful and common use case and the implementation
should be quite trivial following the lead of the FOPEN_DIRECT_IO
per-file opt-out.

Thanks,
Amir.

reviews.author: Joanne Koong (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledged that mmap operations bypass passthrough checks, agreed with Amir's suggested model for handling reads and writes in passthrough mode, and confirmed that writes are always forwarded directly to the server.
reviews.raw_body:
Agh that's right, mmap directly operates on the page cache. The
corrected model for passthrough read + non-passthrough write is then:
* reads and mmaps are always passed through, writes are always
forwarded directly to the server

Thanks,
Joanne

2026-04-22.patch_summary: This patch adds passthrough operation gating to the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) code. It introduces a check for whether passthrough operations can be called based on the current file's mode and the FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO flag. The check is only performed when this flag is enabled, preserving backwards compatibility with previous passthrough behavior. The patch modifies several existing callers to use this new gating mechanism, ensuring that passthrough operations are only attempted when permitted. This change aims to improve security and maintainability of the FUSE codebase by providing a more controlled approach to handling passthrough operations.
